name: Allrounder Antiqua
classification_notes: Described explicitly as an old-style serif, rooted in a revival of Robert Granjon's 'Ascendonica Romain' from 1567, placing it firmly in the Renaissance/Garalde tradition.
summary: A Granjon-inspired old-style serif in four weights with true italics, part of the broader Allrounder superfamily designed to pair with Allrounder Grotesk.
description: Rooted in Moritz Kleinsorge's revival study of Robert Granjon's Ascendonica Romain from 1567, Allrounder Antiqua evolved beyond strict historical revival into a contemporary old-style serif with classical proportions. Four weights from Book to Bold come with corresponding true italics and a thorough OpenType feature set including small caps, six figure sets, swash alternates and support for over 200 Latin-based languages. Shared vertical metrics and colour with Allrounder Grotesk make the two families directly interchangeable within the same layout.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Allrounder Antiqua as a timeless old-style serif based on classical proportions, suitable for book and editorial design, packaging and branding. Originating as Moritz Kleinsorge's graduation project at the Plantin Institute for Typography in Antwerp, it began as a revival of Granjon's Ascendonica Romain before evolving into a Granjon-inspired design in its own right. The 2024 v2.0 release overhauled the entire Allrounder superfamily with revised metrics.

name: Allrounder Didone
classification_notes: High contrast between thick and thin strokes with vertical stress and hairline serifs, characteristic of the Didone or Modern serif tradition. The foundry names it explicitly as Didone.
summary: A Didone serif in six weights with two optical sizes, pitched at fashion, culture and science publishing.
description: Part of the Allrounder superfamily, this Didone offers six weights from XLight to Bold, each with a matching italic, across two optical variants: the standard cut for general typesetting and Allrounder Didone Big for display use at larger sizes. The approximately 950-glyph set includes small capitals, oldstyle and lining figures, scientific inferiors, superscripts, subscripts, and an extensive discretionary ligature set. OpenType feature coverage makes it serviceable across editorial, branding and academic contexts where high-contrast serifs are appropriate.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Allrounder Didone as a modern serif from the Allrounder superfamily, suited to fashion, culture and science. The family comprises six weights, around 950 glyphs, and a broad range of OpenType features, with two optical sizes: the standard Allrounder Didone for all-purpose typesetting and Allrounder Didone Big for striking large-scale display work.

name: Allrounder Baroque
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a transitional or 'rococo' serif, drawing on Baroque-era punchcutters such as van Dijck and Fleischman, with medium contrast, cupped serifs and ball terminals characteristic of transitional letterforms.
summary: A transitional serif drawing on Baroque-era punchcutting traditions, designed by Moritz Kleinsorge as part of the broader Allrounder superfamily.
description: Built on the heritage of 17th and 18th-century punchcutters such as Christoffel van Dijck and Joan Michaël Fleischman, the design brings medium contrast, cupped serifs and ball terminals into a contemporary digital context without tipping into excess. Ten styles across five weights with italics cover long-form text, branding and UI work, backed by an OpenType feature set including small caps, multiple figure styles, case-sensitive forms and support for over 200 Latin-based languages. Its membership of the Allrounder superfamily means it can be paired with Allrounder Grotesk or Allrounder Grotesk Mono without metric adjustment.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Allrounder Baroque as a playful and dynamic medium-contrast transitional serif, whose lush shapes are designed to guide the reader's eye rather than merely decorate the page. Designer Moritz Kleinsorge balanced long spikes, cupped serifs and bouncy ball terminals so they add flair without distracting from text. The foundry position it for fiction books, text-heavy websites, UI/UX, luxury packaging and branding in finance, law, health and publishing, noting its 900+ glyphs per style and compatibility with any other member of the Allrounder type system.

name: Allrounder Monument
classification_notes: An all-caps titling typeface with delicate serifs drawn from ancient Roman monumental inscriptions, intended exclusively for display sizes. Its inscriptional origins and caps-only design place it firmly in the display category.
summary: An all-caps titling serif drawn from ancient Roman monumental inscriptions, offered in three weights as part of the broader Allrounder superfamily.
description: Conceived from letterforms carved on Roman columns and monuments over two millennia ago, this typeface translates that classical restraint into a contemporary titling tool. Three weights, Book through Medium, share horizontal metrics with the other Allrounder families, making cross-family combination straightforward. OpenType discretionary ligatures and a stylistic set extend the range of headline treatments available.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Allrounder Monument as an inscriptional, dignified member of the Allrounder superfamily, inspired by ancient Roman lettering that radiates classic charm. They position it as a timeless titling typeface suited to posters, magazines, book covers, packaging and signage, and note that its shared metrics with Allrounder Grotesk, Antiqua, Didone and Baroque make it a versatile component of a cohesive typesetting system. Version 2.0, released in 2024, introduced revised horizontal metrics incompatible with earlier layouts.

name: Allrounder Grotesk Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-pitch typeface built on the neutral modernist letterforms of the Allrounder Grotesk family, placing it squarely in the grotesque mono category rather than typewriter or technical mono traditions.
summary: A fixed-width grotesque in five weights, designed to share vertical metrics and texture with the broader Allrounder superfamily for clean cross-family pairing.
description: Built on the neutral modernist shapes of Allrounder Grotesk, this monospaced companion avoids typewriter nostalgia and sci-fi associations in equal measure. Five weights from Light to Bold cover 580+ glyphs per font, with support for over 200 Latin-based languages via the Underware LatinPlus glyph set. OpenType features include fractions, superiors, boxed and circled arrows, and localised forms, making it viable across editorial, code, branding and correspondence work.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Allrounder Grotesk Mono as a non-proportional typeface that brings the neutral modernist shapes of Allrounder Grotesk into contemporary correspondence, computer code and corporate contexts. They position it as part of a complete typesetting superfamily alongside Allrounder Grotesk, Allrounder Antiqua, Allrounder Didone, Allrounder Baroque and Allrounder Monument, with shared vertical metrics enabling effortless pairing across the range. Graphics and editing are credited to Johannes López Ayala.

name: Allrounder Grotesk
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a neo-grotesk, with neutral, unobtrusive forms suited to both text and display use, consistent with the neo-grotesque tradition of restrained, systematic design.
summary: A neutral neo-grotesque with ten weights and hand-corrected obliques, designed for both text and display use as part of the broader Allrounder superfamily.
description: Ten weights spanning Air to Black, each with 900+ glyphs and carefully corrected obliques, make this a thorough workhorse for both running text and large-scale display work. A condensed sub-family and variable font versions extend the range further. The design prioritises neutrality and utility over typographic personality.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Allrounder Grotesk as a neutral, powerful neo-grotesk and part of the wider Allrounder superfamily, positioning it as an unobtrusive team player as well as an excellent soloist. They highlight its suitability for body text and crisp display use, with ten weights, 900+ glyphs per style and carefully hand-corrected obliques.

name: Baghira
classification_notes: A contemporary serif with wedge-shaped serifs and true italics that allude to calligraphic roots, but deliberately avoids historical models, placing it in the contemporary rather than transitional or old-style category.
summary: A contemporary serif by Christian Gruber with wedge-shaped serifs and true italics, suited to editorial, book and corporate work across print and screen.
description: Baghira carries smoothly drawn curves, precisely placed corners and rectangular dots, with no historical model as its reference. Its wedge serifs create a horizontal drive in running text without drawing attention to themselves, making it practical across body copy and headline sizes. Eight fonts across four weights with matching italics, small caps, multiple figure sets and discretionary ligatures give it sufficient range for single-typeface brand systems.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Baghira as a contemporary serif named after the panther in Kipling's Jungle Book, with an elegant appearance and sharp wedge serifs that guide reading without distraction. Designed by Christian Gruber with art direction by Moritz Kleinsorge, the family spans 8 fonts across 4 weights with nearly 800 characters per style, including small caps, multiple figure sets, circled numbers and arrows. They position it for editorial, book, UI/UX and advertising work, and recommend it particularly for brands requiring a single typeface that performs well across all sizes and media.

name: Cattivo
classification_notes: Cattivo belongs to the Italienne tradition of reverse-contrast slab serifs, where horizontal strokes are bold and verticals are relatively thin, rooted in early 19th-century wood type display faces.
summary: A reverse-contrast slab serif in the Italienne tradition, drawing on 19th-century wood type heritage with contemporary proportions and a full set of independently drawn italics across nine weights.
description: Cattivo reinterprets the Italienne genre with moderated horizontal stress, making it more legible and evenly textured than most period revivals while retaining the frontier character of display wood type. Nine weights from Thin to Black each carry genuine italics with a handwriting-inspired construction, a rarity for this genre, extending the family's usefulness into short body text settings. Around 700 glyphs include oldstyle and lining figures in multiple formats, arrows, circled and boxed numerals, and broad Latin Extended language support.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Cattivo as a frontier-proof slab serif rooted in the Italienne tradition of reverse contrast, drawing on wooden saloon signage and Spaghetti Western visual culture. They position it as a tongue-in-cheek reinterpretation rather than a faithful revival, with modern curves and contemporary proportions that keep it useful well beyond period theming. The foundry highlight its rare Thin weights, independently drawn dynamic italics, and suitability for brand identity, packaging, editorial, poster and screen-based design across categories including food, travel, entertainment and cinema.

name: Campuni
classification_notes: Campuni draws its letterforms from handwritten italic models set upright, giving it the warmth and organic character of a humanist sans rather than a geometric or grotesque approach.
summary: A humanist sans built on upright handwritten italic forms, offered in 36 styles including left-leaning Retalics and an optional curved-stroke Smile variant.
description: Campuni takes handwritten italic letterforms and sets them upright, producing a sans-serif with warmth and personality without resorting to calligraphic affectation. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each paired with a left-leaning oblique called a Retalic, and a secondary Smile subfamily gently curves the straight stroke endings for a softer register. Over 750 glyphs per style are included, with OpenType features covering lining and oldstyle figures, case-sensitive forms and a slashed zero.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Campuni as a font that smiles, modelled on the handwritten forms of italics but drawn upright. Neat and clean yet warm and charismatic, it offers a Smile variant that gently curves straight stroke endings, Retalics as left-leaning obliques, 36 styles in total, and a glyph set of 750 or more characters.

name: Faible
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist typeface with details referencing handwriting, including bulged bowls, a curved R leg, and independently drawn italics with internal dynamic — all hallmarks of humanist sans design.
summary: A good-natured humanist sans in six weights with independently drawn italics, distinguished by bulged bowls and a characterful swooping K.
description: Faible is a humanist sans with handwriting-inflected details — notably the ballooned bowls of B, P and R and a curved R leg — that give it a warmth suited to display work on book covers, posters and packaging. Its italics are not sloped romans but independently designed with their own rhythm. The 12-font family ships with over 600 characters, covering ligatures, old-style figures, case-sensitive forms, arrows and tabular figures for more demanding typographic tasks.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Faible as a good-natured humanist typeface built to radiate optimism in display sizes, titles and headlines. Its design references handwriting through details such as merrily bulged bowls and a playful curved R leg, while the italics were drawn independently rather than simply sloped. Six weights from Thin to Black, each with a corresponding italic, make up the 12-font family, which ships with more than 600 characters and a full suite of OpenType features for the ambitious typographer.

name: Confer Sans
classification_notes: Identity Letters explicitly describe Confer Sans as a humanist sans, drawing on the broad-nib pen or marker for angular stroke endings rather than the neutralised terminals of neogrotesk designs.
summary: A humanist sans in 16 styles that takes angular, uncorrected stroke endings from the broad-nib pen as its defining formal feature.
description: Rather than smoothing terminals to meet a geometric programme, Confer Sans lets stroke endings remain angular in the manner of a broad-nib marker, giving it a candid warmth that sets it apart from neogrotesk neutrality. Eight weights from Thin to Black each come with an italic, and each style carries close to 700 glyphs including ten or more figure sets, boxed and circled numbers and arrows, and support for 100+ languages. The foundry positions it for cultural, educational and branding work where anonymous authority would be the wrong register.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Confer Sans as their answer to the predicament of the sans-serif: a typeface that maintains digital precision while honouring the human act of writing. Guided by the natural flow of the broad-nib pen or marker, it celebrates angular, uncorrected stroke endings rather than correcting them to conform to a design programme. The result, they say, is sophisticated enough for professional on-brand communication yet informal enough to reach people who refuse a cold neogrotesk.

name: Compiler
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Compiler as a humanist sans, distinguished by selective serifs on certain letterforms borrowed from console typeface conventions, placing it in a hybrid humanist category.
summary: A console-inspired humanist sans with selective serif detailing, available in 32 styles across two subfamilies with extensive OpenType support.
description: Designed with legibility and technical character in mind, Compiler draws on the aesthetic of console typefaces to produce a humanist sans with a large x-height and serifs applied selectively to certain letterforms. The family spans eight weights from Thin to Black, each with matching italics, and splits into two subfamilies: the accented Compiler and the plainer Compiler Plain. Each style ships with over 900 glyphs and OpenType features including small capitals, oldstyle figures, discretionary ligatures and a slashed zero.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Compiler as a legible, technical and clear typeface with a hint of retro, positioned as an intermedia workhorse from cyberspace powered by productive OpenType functions. Inspired by console typefaces, it is a humanist sans with a large x-height and striking serifs on certain letters, offered across 16 styles with 900-plus glyphs each and optimised for web use.

name: Flink Neue
classification_notes: Flink Neue is explicitly positioned within the geometric sans-serif tradition, drawing on the Bauhaus-era genre and its later developments. The family includes a dedicated Bauhaus subfamily alongside standard and Text optical variants.
summary: A geometric sans-serif in 54 styles across three subfamilies, spanning standard, Text and Bauhaus cuts with nine weights each.
description: Identity Letters' take on the geometric sans tradition, the family draws on 1920s Bauhaus-era sources and their later descendants. It runs across three subfamilies: a standard cut, a Text optical size optimised for smaller settings, and a Bauhaus variant with alternate letterforms. Each subfamily covers nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, and the package includes small capitals, oldstyle figures, circled and squared figure sets, and a broad range of OpenType alternates.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Flink Neue as their interpretation of the geometric typeface genre, a staple of typographers since the 1920s, inspired by early giants of the form as well as later designers who developed their own legacy within it. The result is characterised by a clean and focused appearance across 54 fonts.

name: Flink Neue Condensed
classification_notes: Explicitly positioned as a geometric sans-serif in the tradition of 1920s geometric typefaces; clean, structured letterforms with Bauhaus-influenced alternates confirm the classification.
summary: A condensed geometric sans in nine weights across three subfamilies, drawing on early 20th-century geometric traditions including a dedicated Bauhaus variant.
description: Flink Neue Condensed is Identity Letters' take on the geometric sans genre, citing influence from both the early giants of the 1920s and later derivatives. The family spans 54 fonts across three subfamilies: standard, Text, and Bauhaus, each running from Thin to Black with matching italics. Glyph coverage is broad, with small caps, oldstyle figures, discretionary ligatures, and a suite of encircled and squared numeral ornaments.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Flink Neue Condensed as their take on geometric typefaces, a staple of the typographer's toolbox since the 1920s. Inspired by early giants of the genre as well as later epigones with legacies of their own, the family is characterised by a clean and focused appearance.

name: Flink Neue Compressed
classification_notes: Explicitly positioned as Identity Letters' take on the geometric sans genre, drawing on Bauhaus-era conventions. Clean, focused construction with circular forms is characteristic of geometric sans classification.
summary: A compressed geometric sans in three subfamilies spanning nine weights with italics, drawing on Bauhaus-era and early twentieth-century geometric traditions.
description: The family splits into three optical subfamilies: a standard display cut, a Text variant optimised for smaller sizes, and a Bauhaus cut with alternate letterforms referencing early modernist conventions. All three share nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, giving 54 fonts in total. OpenType features include small capitals, old-style figures, discretionary ligatures and multiple figure sets.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Flink Neue Compressed as their interpretation of the geometric sans genre, citing the early 1920s giants as well as later derivatives with their own legacy, and characterise the result as clean and focused in appearance.

name: Flink Neue Variable
classification_notes: Explicitly inspired by Futura, Erbar, Kabel and Avant Garde Gothic, Flink Neue sits squarely in the geometric sans tradition, characterised by neat, near-circular forms and a clean, focused appearance.
summary: A geometric sans offered as a single variable font, spanning nine weights from Thin to Black with italics and a notably large x-height relative to its historical references.
description: Drawing on the canon of 1920s geometric sans-serifs, particularly Futura, Erbar and Kabel, and the later influence of Avant Garde Gothic, Flink Neue is Identity Letters' contribution to the genre. Its x-height is larger than most classic geometric sans-serifs, and its proportions are harmonised rather than strictly geometric, broadening its usefulness from body copy to display sizes. The family is issued as a single variable font file and includes small capitals, oldstyle figures, Bauhaus alternates and an extensive set of OpenType features.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Flink Neue as their take on the geometric sans genre, citing Futura, Erbar, Kabel and Avant Garde Gothic as touchstones. They position it as a sibling to Flink Slab, and emphasise its versatility across applications ranging from fine print and body copy to logos and billboard-scale display work, noting its larger-than-typical x-height and harmonised proportions.

name: Flink Slab
classification_notes: Unbracketed slab serifs built on a geometric skeleton make this a clear geometric slab serif; the foundry explicitly describes it as a clean geometric superfamily with unbracketed slab serifs.
summary: A geometric slab superfamily with unbracketed serifs, offered alongside an energetic 'Voltage' alternate set aimed at display use.
description: Built on the same skeleton and vertical metrics as the companion sans Flink Neue, Flink Slab spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with an italic, totalling 36 fonts in the complete package. The Voltage sub-family provides alternate letterforms across all weights and italics, designed to push harder at display sizes. OpenType features include small capitals, oldstyle figures, double-storey 'a', triple-storey 'g' and a slashed zero, giving the family reasonable typographic depth.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Flink Slab as a clean geometric superfamily with unbracketed slab serifs, featuring energetic 'Voltage' alternates for electrifying impact at display sizes. It pairs with Flink Neue, sharing a common skeleton, vertical metrics, matching proportions and the same range of widths.

name: Flink Slab Compressed
classification_notes: Unbracketed slab serifs on a clean geometric skeleton place this firmly in the geometric slab category, distinct from humanist or transitional slab traditions.
summary: A compressed geometric slab superfamily with unbracketed serifs, offered in nine weights with a separate high-energy 'Voltage' sub-family for display use.
description: Thirty-six fonts span two sub-families: the standard Flink Slab Compressed and the Voltage variant, which provides alternate letterforms designed for maximum impact at display sizes. Both share the same geometric skeleton and vertical metrics as Flink Neue, allowing the two superfamilies to be mixed freely across a layout. OpenType features include small capitals, oldstyle figures, proportional figures and alternates such as double-storey a and triple-storey g.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Flink Slab Compressed as a clean geometric superfamily with unbracketed slab serifs, featuring energetic 'Voltage' alternates for electrifying impact at display sizes. It pairs directly with Flink Neue, sharing a common skeleton, vertical metrics, matching proportions and the same range of widths.

name: Flink Slab Condensed
classification_notes: Unbracketed slab serifs on a geometric skeleton place this firmly in the geometric slab category; the condensed width and Voltage display alternates extend its range without changing its structural classification.
summary: A condensed geometric slab superfamily with unbracketed serifs, offered in nine weights with a high-energy 'Voltage' display sub-family.
description: Thirty-six fonts span nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, across two sub-families: the standard condensed cut and the Voltage variant, which introduces expressive alternates tuned for display sizes. The family shares a common skeleton, vertical metrics and proportions with Flink Neue, making the two superfamilies directly interoperable. OpenType features include small capitals, oldstyle figures, alternate single- and double-storey letterforms, and a slashed zero.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Flink Slab Condensed as a clean geometric superfamily with unbracketed slab serifs, featuring energetic Voltage alternates designed for electrifying impact at display sizes. It pairs directly with Flink Neue, sharing a common skeleton, vertical metrics, matching proportions and the same range of widths.

name: Flink Slab VF
classification_notes: Unbracketed slab serifs built on a geometric skeleton, explicitly positioned as the slab companion to a geometric sans, placing it firmly in the geometric slab tradition that followed 1920s geometric sans designs.
summary: A geometric slab superfamily with unbracketed serifs, available as a variable font with an angular display subfamily inspired by Futurism.
description: Built to pair directly with Flink Neue, the family shares the same skeleton, vertical metrics and proportions as its geometric sans companion, making the two interchangeable without manual adjustment. The Voltage subfamily introduces angular, almost brutalist letterforms across 54 styles, drawing on the visual language of early 20th-century Futurism. Each font carries over 1,300 glyphs covering small capitals, multiple figure sets, circled and boxed numbers, arrows and thin punctuation for interface and print use.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Flink Slab VF as a clean geometric superfamily with unbracketed slab serifs, designed as the ideal companion to their geometric sans Flink Neue. The two families share a common skeleton, vertical metrics and the same range of widths. The Voltage subfamily channels the spirit of Futurism with edgy, angular letterforms that take a near-brutalist approach to construction. The font ships with 1,300-plus glyphs, multiple figure sets, circled and boxed numbers, and thin punctuation, and is available as both a variable font and a static superfamily of 108 styles.

name: Foliant Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist typeface with a geometric look and dynamically angled stroke endings, placing it at the intersection of humanist and geometric sans traditions.
summary: A humanist sans with geometric proportions and angled stroke endings, spanning 10 weights across two subfamilies tuned for text and brand use respectively.
description: Foliant Sans pairs a geometric appearance with humanist construction, using dynamically angled stroke endings to keep it approachable and sincere at text sizes. The default subfamily mirrors the letterforms of the companion Foliant Serif for consistent legibility across mixed-type settings, while Foliant Sans Modern strips back detail for a cleaner, more graphic presence at display sizes. Forty-two fonts cover 10 weights from Air to Black, each with matching italics, and include OpenType features such as small capitals, oldstyle figures and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters position Foliant Sans as a humanist typeface built for knowledge work, combining a geometric look with dynamically angled stroke endings for an approachable, legible and sincere character. The default version is designed to mirror Foliant Serif for optimal legibility, while Foliant Sans Modern offers a simpler, more graphic alternative suited to branding at large sizes. The complete family spans 42 fonts with over 1,000 glyphs.

name: Glance Sans
classification_notes: Geometric in construction with a distinctive non-joining stroke principle where strokes intentionally leave visible gaps at stems, creating a quasi-stencil effect without detached serifs.
summary: A geometric sans with a distinctive non-joining stroke detail that creates visible gaps between strokes and stems, giving it a controlled, urban energy across eight weights.
description: Companion to Glance Slab, this family shares the same design principle of intentionally disconnected strokes that stop short of their stems, producing a subtle stencil-like quality without the bulk of detached serifs. Eight weights run from Thin to Black with a full set of individually drawn italics that reference classic italic construction rather than simple slanting. Each style carries 650+ glyphs including six figure sets, circled numerals, circled arrows and dual stylistic versions of currency symbols, making it particularly suited to branding, wayfinding and sports-themed work.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Glance Sans as the urban alter ego of Glance Slab, a strong-willed geometric sans built on the same non-joining stroke principle but with a calmer presence thanks to the absence of detached serifs. They position it for industrial and high-tech branding, wayfinding in contemporary architecture, corporate identity across arts and education, and sports-themed design, noting that its powerful italics make it equally viable as a text face when paired with Glance Slab at display sizes.

name: Foliant Serif
classification_notes: Foliant Serif features a slightly tilted axis with flat, unbracketed and asymmetrically rectangular serifs, placing it outside historical classifications; the foundry describes it as a contemporary design with roots in handwritten Latin letterforms.
summary: A contemporary text serif with flat unbracketed serifs and a slightly tilted axis, designed for sustained reading in both print and digital knowledge work.
description: Spanning five weights with matching italics, the family is built around body copy legibility, with finely tuned serifs intended to carry the eye smoothly through long passages of text. The glyph set runs to over 900 characters, including small capitals, oldstyle and lining figures, tabular sets, superscripts, subscripts and boxed and circled numerals suited to structured, multi-layered documents. At display sizes, clean curves and crisp details give the heavier weights a composed, erudite quality that pairs with Foliant Sans across the superfamily.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Foliant Serif as the text face of the Foliant superfamily, conceived for the knowledge work of today: reports, papers, databases, education platforms and books alike. They drew it with a slightly tilted axis and flat, asymmetrically rectangular serifs that allude to the handwritten origins of Latin letterforms without following any historical model closely, aiming for a typeface that conveys trust, poise and a degree of elegance while remaining neutral enough for any industry.

name: Glance Slab
classification_notes: A slab serif with deliberately detached serifs and disconnected strokes, creating a stencil-like character that sets it apart from conventional slab designs.
summary: A slab serif with floating, disconnected strokes that produce a stencil-like sparkle at display sizes and act as ink traps at smaller settings.
description: The detached serifs and unjoined strokes are its defining feature: at large sizes they create a flickering, stencil-adjacent rhythm, while at body sizes the gaps function as ink traps, maintaining legibility. The 2024 update added italics, a stylistic set for an extreme stencil mode, and grew the glyph count to 730, covering Latin Plus with oldstyle figures, circled numerals and arrow sets. Designed as a companion to Glance Sans, the two families share proportions and can be paired across branding, posters and event signage.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Glance Slab as an experimental design playing on tension between connection and detachment, comparing its character to the elegance of an ice skater combined with the determination of a quarterback. The 2024 version adds italics, an extended character set and a new Stylistic Set 01 that pushes it into extreme stencil territory, while the family has been redrawn to harmonise more closely with its companion, Glance Sans.

name: Kisba Nova Headline
classification_notes: Kisba Nova Headline is a high-contrast display serif combining wedge serifs and spiky spurs with soft ball terminals, drawing on neoclassical stroke contrast traditions while pushed toward expressive, theatrical extremes.
summary: A high-contrast display serif that pairs sharp wedge serifs and spiky spurs with soft ball terminals across seven weights, optimised for use at 24pt and above.
description: The Headline optical size of Kisba Nova pushes the family's neoclassical contrast to its most dramatic register, with thin monolinear diacritics and punctuation that reinforce its contemporary edge. Seven weights from Thin to Black give range across titles and editorial headlines, and around 600 glyphs per weight cover extended Latin with OpenType features including oldstyle and lining figures, discretionary ligatures and case-sensitive forms. The companion Text subfamily handles smaller sizes, keeping the visual character intact at reading scale.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Kisba Nova as a typeface designed to arouse attention, combining sharp wedge serifs and spiky spurs with round ball terminals and neoclassical stroke contrast. The Headline subfamily is optimised for 24pt and above, flaunting flamboyant letterforms in seven weights from Thin to Black, with thin monolinear diacritics and punctuation that add a modern, elegant character.

name: Joie Grotesk
classification_notes: A clean grotesque sans-serif drawing on early 19th-century grotesque conventions, with optional quirky alternates accessible via stylistic sets that soften its otherwise harmonious neutrality.
summary: An 18-style grotesque sans with early 19th-century references and a suite of stylistic alternates for adjusting its character.
description: Nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic and a variable font axis, give the family considerable range for editorial, branding and packaging work. Over a dozen stylistic sets allow fine-grained control of the warmth introduced by historical grotesque details, including single-storey a, triple-storey g and hook variants on d, l and u. The 750-plus glyph count covers Latin Extended with oldstyle figures, case-sensitive forms, discretionary ligatures and full fraction support.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Joie Grotesk as a typeface for everyone, designed to be clean and harmonic while borrowing quirks from early 19th-century grotesques that add warmth. More than a dozen stylistic sets let users fine-tune that character. At 18 styles and over 750 glyphs, the family is pitched as an optimistic voice for company, campaign, publication or packaging use.

name: Kisba Nova Text
classification_notes: The typeface combines wedge serifs and spiky spurs with ball terminals and neoclassical stroke contrast, placing it firmly in the neoclassical or contrast serif tradition, with display-derived DNA adapted for text setting.
summary: A text-optimised optical size of Kisba Nova, pairing sharp wedge serifs and spiky spurs with soft ball terminals across seven weights for body copy between 9 and 18pt.
description: Kisba Nova Text reduces the headline subfamily's dramatic contrast and widens letter proportions and spacing to sustain legibility at smaller sizes without losing the family's defining character. The mix of sharp wedge serifs, spiky spurs and round ball terminals gives it a restless visual energy unusual in a text face. Around 600 characters per weight cover extended Latin and Greek, with OpenType features including oldstyle and lining figures, a large ligature set, case-sensitive forms and discretionary ligatures.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Kisba Nova as a typeface built on visual extremes, combining sharp wedge serifs and spiky spurs with round ball terminals and neoclassical stroke contrast. The Text subfamily addresses legibility for sizes 9 to 18pt by reducing contrast, widening proportions, and using more even punctuation and diacritics, while retaining what they call the true, elegant Kisba DNA. It ships in seven weights from Thin to Black with roughly 600 characters per style.

name: Leifa
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a flare-serif, Leifa sits between Didone high contrast and humanist warmth, with flattened serifs and tapered spurs rather than the strictly geometric bracketing of a Didone.
summary: A flare-serif with higher-than-usual contrast that reads cleanly at text sizes and asserts itself at display scales, across eight weights with matching oblique italics.
description: Leifa sits between Didone drama and humanist approachability, using flattened serifs and deeply incised tapered spurs to soften what might otherwise be cold high-contrast forms. The family spans eight weights from Thin to Black, each with an optically corrected oblique italic, totalling 16 styles. Each style carries 883 glyphs, including small caps, old-style and tabular figures, fractions, case-sensitive forms and four stylistic sets for the a, f, g and punctuation.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Leifa as a flare-serif that balances elegance with an affable character, equally at home in body copy and headline use. They note its higher-than-usual contrast may initially evoke Didone typefaces, but that flattened serifs and incised tapered spurs introduce humanist qualities that become most apparent at display sizes. The full family of 16 styles, with 883 glyphs per style, is positioned for branding and editorial work across fashion, lifestyle, food, health, beauty, architecture and related fields.

name: Mainboard
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sans; spurless construction with generous apertures and smooth, aerodynamic curves places it firmly in the humanist sans tradition rather than geometric or grotesque.
summary: A spurless humanist sans in eight weights with italics, aimed at technology, sports and automotive brands that need legibility across screen and print.
description: Mainboard strips spurs from letters such as 'g', 'n' and 'r' to achieve a smooth, aerodynamic quality suited to high-tech and sports contexts. Generous apertures and rectangular dots support on-screen legibility at small sizes, while the dynamic curves hold up at stadium scale. The family ships with 600-plus glyphs, several stylistic sets including a single-storey 'a' and slashed zero, and pairs with Mainboard Mono for tables and code.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Mainboard as their typographic expression of speed and progress, common to sports, computing and high-performance industries. They highlight its spurless construction for an aerodynamic personality, generous apertures for screen legibility, and rectangular dots as a nod to technical culture. The family of 16 fonts covers eight weights with italics, extensive glyph sets including circled and boxed figures, and is designed to work from PCB board printing up to stadium-scale signage.

name: Layfort
classification_notes: Described as a contrasted sans-serif with old-style proportions and vivid width variation, drawing on both industrial grotesque and Art Déco influences, placing it between a classical grotesque and a stylised display sans.
summary: A contrasted sans-serif drawing on Art Déco and industrial grotesque traditions, with old-style letter-width variation and true italics narrower than their upright counterparts.
description: Layfort pairs the directness of an industrial grotesque with the swagger of Art Déco styling, producing a contrasted sans that sits between fashion editorial and serious corporate work. The varying letter widths give text a livelier texture than a typical contemporary sans, and the true italics are genuinely narrower rather than simply slanted. Thirty-two fonts span eight weights with matching italics across two subfamilies, the Plain variant offering an alternative detail set, and 750 glyphs cover extended Latin with oldstyle figures, discretionary ligatures and circled or squared ornamental sets.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Layfort as the result of crossing Industrial Revolution with Art Déco — the raw force of steam-powered vessels with the panache of streamliners. It is a contrasted sans-serif with old-style proportions, where varying letter widths create a more vivid texture than a typical contemporary sans and the true italics are narrower than the uprights. At 16 styles and 750 glyphs it is built for complex typographic demands, and is positioned as elegant enough for fashion, art and luxury while remaining sincere enough for serious business.

name: Mainboard Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface derived from a spurless humanist sans-serif base, with slab-like serifs applied selectively to maintain texture at fixed widths. Classified as mono due to its primary monospaced construction and coding-environment focus.
summary: A spurless humanist monospaced typeface in seven weights, conceived for coding environments and large-format sports and technology applications.
description: Built from a sleek spurless sans with generous apertures and rectangular dots, Mainboard Mono targets on-screen legibility at small sizes whilst retaining enough character to work at stadium scale. Selective slab serifs maintain even texture at monospaced widths, and 500+ glyphs include stylistic alternates, slashed zero and a full suite of figure sets. It pairs directly with Mainboard, the proportional variant within the same superfamily.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Mainboard Mono as part of a superfamily built around speed and progress in computing and sports. Developed from a spurless sans with generous apertures and rectangular dots, it is conceived as a humanist monospaced typeface suited to coding environments, high-tech campaigns, automotive and sports publishing. The lack of spurs on characters like 'g', 'n' and 'r' gives letters an aerodynamic quality, and the family's dual nature is designed to read well both at small screen sizes and at large-format display scales such as stadium banners or vehicle livery.

name: Nomos Sans
classification_notes: Identity Letters describe it as a low-contrast neogrotesk with raw, functional letterforms and blunt junctions between diagonal and vertical stems, placing it squarely in the neogrotesque tradition with a brutalist inflection.
summary: A low-contrast neogrotesque with brutalist sensibilities, built across 18 static styles and a variable axis spanning Thin to Black with matching italics.
description: Nomos Sans draws on both classic modernism and internet-age aesthetics, using simple letterforms and blunt junctions between diagonal and vertical stems to achieve its raw quality. The family ships with over 1000 characters and an extensive set of OpenType alternates, including single- and double-storey 'a' forms, alternate 'l', 'I', 'J', 'y' and 'Y', square punctuation, and both proportional and oldstyle figures. Variable font files cover the full weight range in upright and italic, making the family suited to screen-first work where flexibility across weights is needed.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Nomos Sans as their take on the brutalist genre, a low-contrast neogrotesk with raw aesthetics rooted in classic modernism and the internet age. Simple, functional letterforms and the blunt convergence of diagonal and vertical stems define its character, with 18 styles and a character set exceeding 1000 glyphs.

name: Nomos Slab
classification_notes: A low-contrast slab serif with blunt, angular construction that merges grotesque proportions with slab serifs, rooted in modernist and brutalist aesthetics rather than classical serif traditions.
summary: A low-contrast brutalist slab serif with 18 styles and a variable axis, built around blunt diagonal-to-vertical stem junctions and simple, functional letterforms.
description: Part of the Nomos superfamily, this slab variant takes a brutalist approach with minimal stroke contrast and abrupt stem transitions that reference both classic modernism and contemporary digital aesthetics. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, plus full variable font support covering the entire weight range in both uprights and italics. Over 1,000 characters cover Latin Extended with OpenType features including small capitals, oldstyle figures, proportional figures and alternative single-storey forms.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Nomos Slab as part of their take on the brutalist genre, with raw aesthetics built on an orderly construction drawing from classic modernism and the internet age. Simple, functional letterforms and blunt diagonal-to-vertical stem convergence define its character, delivered as a low-contrast workhorse across 18 styles with a 1,000-plus character set.

name: Portheras
classification_notes: Identity Letters explicitly describe Portheras as a humanist sans; its low x-height, informal character and italics that sit between oblique and true italic confirm that classification.
summary: A fairly wide humanist sans with a relaxed disposition, named after a Cornish beach and built around British design tradition with an informal edge.
description: Sixteen styles span Thin to Black with matching italics that fall somewhere between oblique and true italic, giving the family a character that is approachable without being casual in a careless way. Each style carries 780 glyphs covering small caps, multiple figure sets including old-style figures at mid-cap height, three stylistic sets and an extensive arrow collection. The low x-height and slightly wider stance set it apart from more utilitarian humanist sans designs.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Portheras as their answer to "smart casual" in type form: a fairly wide contemporary humanist sans inspired by the Cornish beach of Portheras Cove, paying homage to British design tradition while incorporating an informal idiom. The family offers 16 styles with 780 glyphs each and OT features including small caps, numerous figure sets, arrows and three stylistic sets.

name: Werksatz
classification_notes: Explicitly inspired by Akzidenz Grotesk and Venus, two canonical 19th-century grotesques; the design follows their low-contrast, upright, industrial warmth characteristic of the genre.
summary: A contemporary grotesque drawing on Akzidenz Grotesk and Venus, offered across 10 weights with obliques and a variable font.
description: Built in the tradition of early European grotesques, Werksatz takes the low-contrast, industrially warm character of Akzidenz Grotesk and Venus and redraws it for present-day use. The family spans 10 weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching oblique, and includes a variable font covering the full weight axis. Over 940 characters per style give it broad Latin Extended coverage including small capitals, oldstyle figures and a comprehensive set of OpenType features.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Werksatz as a contemporary interpretation of early grotesque typefaces, citing Akzidenz Grotesk and Venus as its primary references. They frame these classics as timeless designs that designers rediscover generation after generation, and position Werksatz as an adaptation that takes the best from those originals and updates them for modern tools and technology.

name: Werkdruck
classification_notes: The foundry describes it as threading together rational and transitional type design sources, with vertical stress and symmetrical construction akin to a Didone but with reduced stroke contrast and bracketed serifs, placing it firmly in transitional territory.
summary: A nine-weight transitional serif drawing on rational and Didone traditions, optimised for legibility at small sizes on high-resolution screens.
description: Werkdruck threads together rational and transitional type design references, adopting the vertical stress and symmetrical construction of a Didone while softening the stroke contrast and retaining bracketed serifs. Nine weights run from Thin to Heavy, each with true italics, and alternate glyph options include single-storey and double-storey variants for a and g. Its 911 glyphs per style cover extended Latin and include small capitals, oldstyle figures and a full suite of superscripts and subscripts.
foundry_blurb: Identity Letters describe Werkdruck as a serif typeface from the good old days made fit for the internet age, with a no-nonsense concept rooted in rational and transitional design from a time when honest work was in style. Its sturdy construction keeps body copy efficient and easy to read, while digitally precise curves hold up well at display sizes. Designed as a companion to their sans-serif Werksatz.
